Title: EPF rejects Sunway's IJM takeover offer: Spokesperson
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The Employees Provident Fund (EPF) has decided to decline the takeover offer by Sunway Bhd for its shares in IJM Corp Bhd, according to an unnamed spokesperson.
EPF, which holds a 20.52 per cent stake in IJM, becomes the second government-linked investment fund to reject Sunway's offer, following Permodalan Nasional Bhd (PNB), which holds 13.3 per cent.
PNB said on March 16 that its investment committee had decided not to accept the offer, citing IJM's intrinsic value and potential future gains relative to Sunway's share price.
Other investment funds with interests in IJM include Kumpulan Wang Persaraan Diperbadankan (9.26 per cent) and Lembaga Tabung Haji (1.41 per cent).
An independent advice circular released earlier on March 13 to IJM shareholders by M&A Securities Sdn Bhd stated that the offer was not fair and not reasonable.
The investment banking outfit had recommended that shareholders reject the offer.
IJM, which reported an order book of RM15.3 billion as of end-December 2025 and cash reserves of RM2.3 billion, has been underperforming compared with peers such as Gamuda Bhd and Sunway Construction Bhd.
On Jan 12, Sunway offered RM3.15 per IJM share through a combination of cash and share exchange.
The cash portion represents 10 per cent of the offer, with the balance to be settled via Sunway shares priced at RM5.65 each.
Sunway shareholders approved the proposal last week, though EPF abstained from the vote, citing its holdings in both IJM and Sunway.
The offer is conditional on Sunway acquiring more than 50 per cent of IJM. As of now, acceptances stand just below 13 per cent, with the first closing date set for April 6.
Sunway has 60 days from dispatch of the offer documents to complete the transaction, meaning the offer period could be extended.
Following the announcement, IJM commissioned two independent valuations that placed its fair value well above Sunway's offer.
M&A Securities valued IJM shares between RM5.84 and RM6.48, while Rothschild & Co Malaysia Sdn Bhd valued the company between RM4.80 and RM5.63 per share.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: A-GC rejects representation, Chinese duo to face trial over Mindef trespass
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Two Chinese nationals accused of trespassing into a restricted zone at the Defence Ministry will stand trial after the Attorney-General's Chambers rejected their representations.
Defence lawyer, Ooi Hong Ming, who represents photographers Li Luorong, 28, and Chen Junhao, 21, said deputy public prosecutor Nor Aisyah Mohamed Zanyuin
informed Sessions Court judge Suhaila Haron of the decision during recent proceedings.
"The prosecution has requested for another case management date to make sure everything is in order before the case proceeds to trial.
"The next case management is set for Thursday," he said.
The accused were denied bail after they were charged last year.
On the first charge, Li and Chen are jointly accused of flying a DJI Mavic 3 Enterprise drone to capture aerial footage in a gazetted prohibited area without authorisation with the intent of acquiring official information detrimental to national security.
The incident allegedly occurred at 11.50am at the ministry's Padang Bola B area in Jalan Padang Tembak on Aug 6.
The offence under Section 3(a) of the Official Secrets Act read with Section 34 of the Penal Code carries a life imprisonment upon conviction.
Section 3(a) deals with any person for any purpose prejudicial to the safety or interest of Malaysia approaches, inspects, passes over or is in the neighbourhood of, or enters any prohibited place.
On the second charge, they are accused of using the same drone to capture aerial footage for surveying purposes without obtaining prior authorisation from the authorities at the same time, location and date.
The charge under Section 4(1) of the act read with Section 34 of the code provides a jail term of between one year and 14 years if found guilty.
Section 4(1) is when a person takes or makes any document, measurement, sounding or survey of or within a prohibited area.
Section 34 reads a criminal act done by several persons with common intention.
On Aug 15, Wangsa Maju police chief Senior Assistant Commissioner Mohammad Lazim Ismail said the duo were caught by Military Police while operating a drone on a football field near Wisma Pertahanan.
Lazim had said preliminary investigations revealed that the pair were instructed by their employer to record the drone footage at the site but were unaware of the actual purpose.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: A maths book, a suspected drunk driver, a father lost
Article:
LARUT: The father of three who was  killed by a suspected drunk driver in Klang yesterday was on his way to buy a schoolbook for his child, who was preparing to begin the school term.
Nor Nadia Abdul Majid, 32, said her husband, Amirul Hafiz Omar, 33, had left home at about 10am to go to Section 19 to purchase a Mathematics book for their eldest child.
"He said he wanted to buy a Mathematics book for our child, as he had been planning to do so for several days.
"The last time we communicated was a few minutes before 11am, when he sent a message asking about some documents.
"At 11am, he messaged saying an item had arrived and after that, I could no longer reach him," she said when met at her home in Kampung Repoh, Batu Kurau, here.
She said she later received a call from a traffic police officer informing her that her husband had been involved in an accident.
"I thought the call was a scam, but when he mentioned my husband's name, I immediately felt uneasy.
"He told me my husband had passed away, and at that moment I just couldn't accept it. My heart was shattered," she said in tears.
Nor Nadia said she intends to take legal action against those involved and has already engaged a lawyer.
"I want justice for my husband and my children. I don't want this case to just pass by. My family and I strongly hope for stricter punishment for drunk drivers to prevent such incidents from happening again.
"The current laws are not enough to make up for my husband's life," she said.
[[nid:1406661]]
She now faces the challenge of raising their three children, aged between two and nine, who do not yet understand the loss of their father.
"My children think their father is sick, they don't understand yet. I have to be strong for them," she said.
Meanwhile, the victim's mother, Faridah Ahmad, 56, recalled their final moments together during a recent family gathering in Klang.
"When we met relatives during the gathering, he joked that one day his photo would appear in the newspapers and on television. But we never expected that joke to become reality.
"Before we left to return to Batu Kurau, he hugged me and apologised for being too busy earning a living for the family.
"I told him it was okay, that I understood. But I never imagined that it would be our last meeting," she said.
She said she has accepted the tragedy and hopes authorities will carry out a thorough investigation and impose a heavy punishment on the perpetrator, as her son died innocently.
He was laid to rest last night at the Kampung Repoh Muslim Cemetery.
In the 11.40am incident, Amirul, who worked as a delivery rider was killed after a car driven by a man in his 20s crashed into his motorcycle.
The driver is believed to have overtaken several vehicles before entering the opposite lane and colliding with Amirul's motorcycle.
The driver, who has been remanded for four days, has tested positive for both alcohol and drugs.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Police seek charges in Wangsa Maju 'protection money' case
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Police have referred investigation papers involving two men allegedly demanding "protection money" from a massage parlour in Wangsa Maju to the Deputy Public Prosecutor (DPP), recommending that charges be filed.
Wangsa Maju police chief Senior Assistant Commissioner Mohamad Lazim Ismail said the papers were submitted today.
"We handed it over to the DPP with recommendations to charge both suspects. I will not reveal the recommendations at this point," he told reporters.
MORE TO COME
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 1,085 Kada padi farmers affected by drought across six districts
Article:
KOTA BARU: A total of 1,085 paddy farmers under Kemubu Agricultural Development Authority (Kada) are affected by the ongoing drought in several major districts in Kelantan.
Kada chairman Khalid Abdul Samad said the affected areas involved six districts, namely Kota Baru Utara (KBU), Kota Baru Selatan (KBS), Pasir Puteh, Bachok, Pasir Mas and Tumpat.
He said the total area affected covers 3,638 hectares, involving various levels of impact on paddy crops.
"Several immediate measures have been implemented by Kada to mitigate the impact of the drought on rice production.
"Among them is the operation of the Kemubu pump 4, which began in early February 2026 and has been effective in ensuring sufficient water supply to paddy fields," he told reporters when met at his office here today.
He said Kemubu Pump 4 is able to operate at a minimum water level of a metre, compared with Kemubu Pump 1 and 2, which require higher water levels of 4m and 3.5m respectively.
"Currently, the water level of Kelantan River stands at 2.10m as of March 27, 2026," he said.
Khalid said Kada has also distributed support pumps to channel water from secondary and tertiary irrigation canals to paddy plots, in addition to supplying diesel to farmers based on allocated quotas.
"We will continue to monitor the situation closely and ensure all assistance is delivered effectively to safeguard rice production and the welfare of farmers," he added.
Meanwhile, commenting on the plight of more than 10 farmers who suffered losses of nearly RM1 million, Khalid said the affected area in Kampung Belukar, Tanjung Puri, faced water supply constraints due to uneven distribution.
"Most of the area is supplied with water, but there are isolated pockets where water does not reach," he said.
He said overall, Kada irrigation coverage currently stands at about 15 per cent.
"If Kemubu 3 is completed, it can help overcome the issue. To achieve 100 per cent coverage, we need Kemubu 2, 3 and 4 fully in operation.
"At present, we are using Kemubu 4, while Kemubu 2 can be operated at night," he said.
Khalid added that Kada's planning is in place but delays in project completion by contractors had affected implementation timelines.
"Once completed, these issues can be resolved," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Thunderstorm warning for KL, 12 states until 6pm
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The Malaysian Meteorological Department (MetMalaysia) has issued a thunderstorm warning at 3.30pm today for Kuala Lumpur and 12 states, with heavy rain and strong winds expected until 6pm.
The warning covers the whole of Perlis and Melaka, as well as parts of Kedah, including Kubang Pasu, Padang Terap, Sik and Baling. In Perak, affected areas stretch from Kerian to Muallim.
In the east coast, several districts in Kelantan, Terengganu and Pahang are also expected to experience adverse weather, including Jeli, Kuala Krai, Gua Musang, Besut, Hulu Terengganu, Cameron Highlands, Lipis, Raub and Bentong.
In the Klang Valley, the advisory includes Selangor's Hulu Selangor, Gombak, Petaling and Hulu Langat districts, as well as Kuala Lumpur.
The warning also extends to Negeri Sembilan and Johor, affecting areas from Tangkak to Johor Baru.
In East Malaysia, heavy rain is forecast in parts of Sarawak, including Kuching, Serian, Samarahan, Sri Aman, Betong, Sarikei, Mukah and Kapit, as well as Sabah's Interior and West Coast divisions.
MetMalaysia said such warnings are issued when rainfall intensity is expected to exceed 20mm per hour for more than an hour, and are valid for up to six hours.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Tamparuli river getting drier after no rain for almost a month
Article:
TAMPARULI: The water level of the Tamparuli River, which is the main source for two Telibong Water Treatment Plants, has dropped significantly due to hot weather and the absence of rain in this district for nearly a month.
Observations found that the river near the Tamparuli bridge has become shallow, with sandbanks forming along the riverbanks, indicating a significant drop in water levels.
This phenomenon of the Tamparuli river becoming shallow usually occurs only during prolonged dry seasons, leading to reduced water levels when there is no heavy rainfall over an extended period.
It is understood that the Telibong water treatment plants are currently only able to supply about 25 to 30 per cent of treated water to consumers in Tuaran and northern Kota Kinabalu.
Tamparuli State Assemblyman, Datuk Seri Wilfred Madius Tangau, said the Telibong Water Treatment Plant should be capable of producing 80 million litres of water per day for consumers in Tuaran and northern Kota Kinabalu.
"If the drought continues and affects the water level of the Tamparuli River, water rationing for consumers will be unavoidable," he said today.
He added that currently, a large portion of residents in Tamparuli and Tuaran are facing treated water supply issues, forcing the state and federal governments to take various approaches to resolve the problem.
In this regard, he advised residents in dozens of housing areas to always conserve water usage and to store water to meet daily needs.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: LPP staffer charged with RM141,872 misappropriation of oil palm produce
Article:
JOHOR BARU: A staff member of the Farmers' Organisation Authority (LPP) was charged in the Sessions Court here with 16 counts of dishonestly misappropriating property worth RM141,872.67 belonging to the Kota Tinggi Timur Area Farmers' Organisation.
Muhammad Faiz Ibrahim, 36, an assistant economic affairs officer, pleaded not guilty after the charges were read before Judge Datuk Ahmad Kamal Arifin Ismail.
The accused, who also served as the organisation's farm manager, is alleged to have dishonestly converted oil palm fresh fruit bunches valued at RM141,872.67 for his own use.
He was charged under Section 403 of the Penal Code, which carries a sentence of between six months and five years' imprisonment, and may also include caning and a fine upon conviction.
Faiz also pleaded not guilty to 16 alternative charges of criminal breach of trust, for allegedly selling the entrusted oil palm produce and depositing the proceeds into his own account.
The alternative charges were framed under Section 406 of the Penal Code, which provides for imprisonment of up to 10 years, and may also include caning and a fine.
All the offences are alleged to have been committed between April 22, 2021, and Nov 15, 2022, at the Kota Tinggi Timur Area Farmers' Organisation, Pusat Kemajuan Peladang Mawai Baru.
At the proceedings, Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission (MACC) prosecuting officer Alice Voon Mei Ling proposed bail of RM25,000 with one surety.
Defence counsel Asiah Abd Jalil sought a lower amount, stating that her client, who has served LPP for 15 years, earns RM4,000 a month and had cooperated fully with investigators since November 2024.
She added that the accused, who is single and the sixth of seven siblings, supports his retired parents, and has since been transferred to another branch. She also noted that the proceedings may result in his salary being withheld.
The court allowed bail at RM10,000 with one surety and set April 30 for submission of documents.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Penang schools beat the heat: Classes go on with safety first
Article:
GEORGE TOWN: The state Education Department will continue face-to-face schooling despite the ongoing heatwave, prioritising the health and well-being of students across 398 schools in Penang.
Its director Mohamad Dziauddin Mat Saad said the decision was made after assessing prevailing weather conditions and the readiness of schools, which have implemented various measures to cope with the heat.
He said that engagement sessions had been held with schools and district education offices to ensure preparedness for any sudden changes in weather.
"Schools have undertaken several initiatives, including advising parents for students to bring sufficient drinking water, allowing more comfortable attire, and ensuring adequate water supply at canteens.
"This morning, I visited Sekolah Kebangsaan Sungai Gelugor and found the school had made the necessary preparations. Students brought their own water, while the school ensured the canteen was supplied and classrooms were well-ventilated.
"We have also advised students to avoid prolonged exposure to heat, drink plenty of water, and immediately inform teachers if they feel unwell.
"As for PdPR (home-based teaching and learning), it remains a last resort, as we want to minimise learning loss and maintain normal schooling wherever possible," he told reporters at the school today.
Dziauddin said the department was in close contact with the Malaysian Meteorological Department (MetMalaysia) for daily weather updates, adding that Penang was no longer at alert level following recent rainfall.
Parts of the state experienced showers yesterday afternoon and in the early hours of this morning.
"We remain cautious as conditions may change. Outdoor activities, including sports, are being monitored to ensure safety.
"Physical education and co-curricular activities may continue but should take place in covered areas or early in the morning to avoid extreme heat," he said.
He added that the department had directed immediate inspections of school facilities, including fans and ventilation systems, to ensure student comfort.
"I have instructed the infrastructure and procurement divisions to identify schools with issues so that prompt action can be taken," he said.
Meanwhile, schools are encouraged to hold special prayers for rain as part of students' educational experience during the week.
"We want to assure parents that our top priority is the health, welfare, and safety of students. With these safeguarded, we are confident learning can proceed in a more conducive environment," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Hot weather: Falling water levels at Lake Beris disrupt daily activities
Article:
SIK: Prolonged hot weather has lowered the level of Lake Beris, affecting daily life and posing challenges for small-scale tourism operators.
Resort operator Abdul Rashid Abdullah, 65, said the current hot spell is among the worst he has experienced in over 25 years of running a business in the area.
"If the hot weather continues, activities here, especially those on the water, like kayaking, will definitely be affected," he told Bernama today.
He explained that the receding water since January has left a floating café stranded on dry ground.
"Areas that were once underwater are now completely exposed. You can even see tree stumps that used to be submerged. The view looks completely different now," he said.
Resident Muhammad Helmi Hashim, 42, who planted about 45 durian saplings of various varieties, as well as cash crops, in the area six months ago, said the situation is affecting activities that rely on the lake for irrigation.
"This area used to be a rubber plantation, and we depended on the lake to irrigate our crops. When the water level drops, we have to move the pump every day to make sure the plants get enough water," he said.
He added that the extended dry spell, combined with water being released from the dam into the river, is among the main reasons for the lake's receding water.
Muhammad Helmi said that if the situation persists and the lake dries up, they may have to rely on water delivered by tanker lorries to protect their crops. – BERNAMA
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Cops arrest five, seize RM25.5mil worth of meth in Perlis
Article:
KANGAR: Police arrested five individuals, including a married couple, and seized 510 kilogrammes (kg) of methamphetamine worth an estimated RM25.5 million in three separate raids in Wang Kelian, Kaki Bukit near Padang Besar last week.
Bukit Aman Narcotics Criminal Investigation Department director Datuk Hussein Omar Khan said the suspects — four men and one woman aged between 23 and 37 — are believed to be members of a drug trafficking syndicate that has been active for several years.
Hussein said police arrested the married couple and a male suspect from two cars they were travelling in along the main road in Wang Kelian during the first raid on March 24.
"Further inspection of the car driven by one of the male suspects led police to seize 20 sacks containing 500 plastic packets filled with substances suspected to be methamphetamine," he said at a press conference at the Perlis police contingent headquarters here today.
He said police were forced to fire several shots at one of the vehicles as the driver's actions posed a threat to the raiding officers and risked causing casualties.
Hussein said the arrest of the three individuals led to the capture of two more male suspects in two separate raids around Wang Kelian on March 27 and 28.
"Preliminary urine screening found the four male suspects positive for methamphetamine, while checks on criminal records showed all five suspects have prior offences involving drugs," he said.
He said all of them have been remanded for between five and seven days from March 25 and yesterday, and the case is being investigated under Section 39B of the Dangerous Drugs Act 1952.
Hussein said action is also being taken under the Dangerous Drugs (Forfeiture of Property) Act 1988, with total seizures amounting to RM566,200, involving six vehicles, including pickup trucks, and eight motorcycles.
On the syndicate, Hussein said it is among the drug trafficking groups under police watch and is believed to have expertise in smuggling drugs via the border route.
"This syndicate is one of the active groups operating in Perlis, believed to smuggle drugs through forested areas in Wang Kelian using human couriers.
"The drugs are brought to a transit point near the main road in Wang Kelian to be collected by those arrested before being sent to other transit points.
The supply is believed to be for the local market, the Klang Valley and the southern region," he said.
He said the seized drugs could have harmed up to 2.55 million users if they had reached the market.
Hussein added that the syndicate leader, a local man in his 40s, is believed to have fled to Thailand, and police will seek assistance from authorities there to track him down. — BERNAMA
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Steering Malaysia through global shockwaves with collective strength
Article:
AT a time when global tensions are once again testing the fragility of economic stability, particularly through evolving geopolitical fault lines involving the United States, Israel, and Iran, Malaysia stands at a decisive policy juncture.
The question is no longer whether external shocks will reach our shores, but how effectively we respond when they do.
The central argument is straightforward: targeted subsidies under the government are not merely a fiscal recalibration, but a strategic imperative to safeguard national resilience, preserve social cohesion, and sustain long-term economic credibility in an increasingly uncertain global environment.
The leadership of Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim has signalled a deliberate shift away from broad-based subsidies toward a more targeted, data-driven framework anchored in national identity systems.
This transition does not occur in isolation. It unfolds against a backdrop of heightened global energy volatility, persistent supply chain disruptions, and rising geopolitical risk premiums that inevitably translate into domestic inflationary pressures.
In such conditions, maintaining blanket subsidies would not only strain fiscal capacity but also dilute the precision of assistance intended for those most in need.
Yet policy rationale alone does not capture the full picture. The lived experience of Malaysians is far more nuanced.
Asnaf and B10 communities continue to confront immediate survival pressures, where even marginal increases in food and transport costs can disrupt daily consumption.
The B40 and Lower M40 remain acutely sensitive to cost-of-living pressures, particularly in urban settings where housing, utilities, and education expenses accumulate rapidly.
Meanwhile, the Upper M40 and broader middle-income groups are increasingly experiencing what may be described as income compression, where earnings growth fails to keep pace with rising expenditures, gradually eroding financial security.
These pressures extend beyond income classifications. The disabled (OKU) communities face persistent structural barriers to access; young people navigate uncertain employment pathways; the elderly contend with fixed incomes amid rising healthcare costs; and women, particularly single-parent households, balance economic responsibilities alongside caregiving roles.
Microentrepreneurs and SMEs operate within an environment of fluctuating input costs, while gig economy workers and informal sector participants often lack stable income buffers.
Even among higher-income groups, concerns regarding asset preservation, currency exposure, and investment stability are becoming more pronounced.
Within this broader context, targeted subsidies must be understood not as a withdrawal of support, but as a refinement of national protection mechanisms.
By linking assistance to verified data through national identity systems, the government seeks to ensure that fiscal resources are allocated with greater accuracy and impact.
This approach becomes particularly critical when external shocks such as disruptions to global energy supply routes or abrupt currency fluctuations can rapidly amplify domestic cost pressures.
The immediate test, however, lies in execution.
Policy credibility will be measured not by announcements, but by outcomes. Any deficiencies in data accuracy, delays in disbursement, or perceptions of inequity in eligibility could erode public confidence.
Transparency, therefore, is not optional but essential. Clear communication of eligibility criteria, accessible grievance mechanisms, and timely updates on subsidy distribution are fundamental to sustaining trust.
Equally important is the need to temper public reaction.
Historical experience suggests that both markets and societies often react more sharply to uncertainty than to underlying fundamentals.
While global tensions are real, Malaysia's macroeconomic foundations remain intact, supported by a stable banking system, a manageable inflation trajectory, and ongoing policy reforms.
The appropriate response is not alarm, but informed vigilance.
At the household level, practical steps can enhance resilience.
Prioritising essential expenditure, managing debt prudently, and building even modest financial buffers can provide meaningful stability.
For businesses, particularly SMEs, diversification of supply chains, disciplined cash flow management, and adoption of digital efficiencies will be increasingly important.
Community-based support structures, including cooperatives and local networks, can further mitigate the impact on vulnerable groups.
This period also underscores the importance of reaffirming social cohesion.
Economic strain, if left unaddressed, risks translating into broader social fragmentation. Malaysia's enduring strength lies in its capacity to navigate complexity through collective understanding and mutual support.
From a policy standpoint, continued refinement of data infrastructure, improved inter-agency coordination, and accelerated digital inclusion are essential to ensure no segment of society is excluded.
Strengthening collaboration between the public and private sectors, particularly in areas such as food security, logistics, and essential services, will further enhance national resilience.
Over time, targeted subsidies must be complemented by structural reforms aimed at improving productivity, raising wages, and expanding economic participation across all segments.
Ultimately, targeted subsidies are not a flawless solution, but they represent a necessary evolution. In a global environment where external shocks are increasingly frequent and unpredictable, fiscal precision is no longer optional. Malaysia must ensure that every ringgit deployed delivers maximum value to those who need it most.
Calm, clarity, and collective discipline will define how effectively the nation navigates the months ahead.
Malaysians are not being asked to endure uncertainty passively, but to respond with informed adaptability. With steady leadership, transparent governance, and a unified society, Malaysia can move forward shaped by global turbulence, yet not destabilised by it.
* The writer is a political and economic analyst
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Taiping Clock Tower chimes again after years of silence
Article:
TAIPING: After years of silence, the iconic Taiping Clock Tower is ringing again, breathing new life into the town's historic centre.
Once known as The Fort, the clock tower has stood for generations. The original timber structure was built in 1881 before being rebuilt in brick in 1891. Between 1908 and 1950, it served as both a town police station and a fire station.
The tower has now been refurbished as a tourist information centre, combining history, culture and sustainability.
State Tourism, Industry, Investment and Corridor Development Committee chairman Loh Sze Yee said the upgrade forms part of a broader effort to strengthen Taiping's tourism infrastructure.
"This clock tower has served multiple purposes over the years, including as a fire station and a police station.
"Now, it has been revitalised to provide information for tourists, especially with the introduction of the Taiping Peace Trails," he said at the launch today.
Loh said the refurbishment cost RM60,000 and involved repairs and improvements to the structure.
"This initiative is a collaborative effort involving the Habitat Foundation, the Finance Ministry, the Taiping Municipal Council, and local tourism associations.
"We hope this becomes a key destination where visitors can access information and better appreciate Taiping's heritage," he added.
He also said that the clock tower's chimes had been silent prior to the refurbishment.
"Visitors can now enjoy both the restored building and the surrounding tourism trails as part of efforts to enhance the town's appeal," he said.
Taiping Heritage Society president Liew Suet Fun said the focus was on improving the quality of narratives presented to visitors.
"As a heritage society, we believe the most important aspect is the quality of storytelling.
"The clock tower sits at the heart of the town, where many tourists pass through, making it an ideal place to share information," she said.
Liew said the refurbishment highlights Taiping's natural and cultural heritage, including the Lake Gardens and surrounding mangroves.
"We also share stories of industries such as charcoal and mangrove-based livelihoods, showing how the landscape supports human life.
"This is especially important today, as we are losing natural habitats," she said.
She added that the upgraded interpretation centre now features 3D models and videos, incorporating themes of regeneration and sustainability.
"We want visitors to experience Taiping meaningfully — not just as a holiday destination, but to understand the values that define our town," she said.
Liew said the centre had previously functioned as an interpretation space, but its materials had become outdated.
"It wasn't dilapidated, but it needed refreshing to reflect current values, particularly sustainable tourism," she said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Thunderstorms, strong winds to hit Selangor, Negri Sembilan and Sarawak
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Thunderstorms, heavy rain and strong winds are expected to lash parts of Selangor, Negri Sembilan and Sarawak until 4pm today.
The Malaysian Meteorological Department (MetMalaysia) issued the warning at 1pm, stating that Hulu Langat in Selangor is likely to be affected.
In Negri Sembilan, the alert covers the districts of Jelebu, Seremban, Port Dickson, Kuala Pilah and Rembau.
Meanwhile, in Sarawak, areas expected to experience adverse weather include Kuching, Serian, Samarahan, Sri Aman, Betong and Daro in Mukah.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Motorcyclist killed in drink-driving crash was a father working two jobs
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: A delivery rider
killed
in a collision with a suspected drink-driver on Jalan Raya Barat yesterday worked two jobs to support his family.
Amirul Hafiz Omar, who was in his 30s, worked at a warehouse by day and as a delivery rider by night.
"He was a hard worker. During the day, he worked at a warehouse, and at night he took on part-time work as a delivery rider," said his friend and colleague of 11 years, Illyasa Saipuzzaman.
Illyasa described Amirul as a helpful and friendly person who never complained about his responsibilities as the head of his family.
"He has three children. The eldest is nine, the second is seven, and the youngest is just two years old.
"We just met on Saturday, and everything seemed normal. By Sunday, he was gone," he said when contacted.
Illyasa also said Amirul was always willing to lend a hand to those in need.
"He would be among the first to step forward."
In the 11.40am incident, Amirul was killed after a car driven by a man in his 20s crashed into his motorcycle.
The driver is believed to have overtaken several vehicles before entering the opposite lane and colliding with Amirul's motorcycle.
The driver, who has been remanded for four days, has tested positive for both alcohol and drugs.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Capital A names Effendy deputy CEO as it enters next growth phase [WATCH]
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Capital A Bhd has appointed Effendy Shahul Hamid as deputy chief executive officer, effective April 6, reinforcing the company's leadership bench as it enters its next phase of growth.
Following the disposal of its aviation businesses to AirAsia X Bhd in January, Capital A is now focused on growing and scaling its other core businesses, namely Asia Digital Engineering, Teleport, AirAsia Move, AirAsia Next and Santan.
Capital A chief executive officer Tan Sri Tony Fernandes said Effendy's experience and leadership style make him a natural fit for the group's next phase of growth.
"He is also a proven operator with a track record that speaks for itself. He understands how ecosystems create value and is able to rally teams around clear goals and deliver real results.
"At this point, we want to go further and faster across all our businesses on the back of digital innovation, and Effendy brings exactly the kind of depth and perspective that we need. I expect him to hit the ground running and add value very quickly," said Fernandes.
Effendy said the opportunity was "too good to pass up".
"The ability to derive value anchored on superior technology use and ecosystem expansion is what I believe the best companies of the future will need to possess.
"I was looking for an opportunity and a place that believed the same thing, and I'm excited that I found it at Capital A," he added.
Effendy said he is looking forward to applying his experience in financial services to help define the group's proposition in this space.
"Its strong ecosystem access and use cases provide an ideal platform to demonstrate what the future could look like in this vertical."
Effendy brings over two decades of regional leadership experience across banking, digital transformation, partnerships and ecosystem building.
During his 21 years at CIMB Group, he ran its regional retail franchise, led its digital banking agenda and drove strategic partnerships and investments, including transforming Touch 'n Go into a leading digital payments platform through its joint venture with Ant Group.
He began his career in corporate development and investment banking, focusing on mergers and acquisitions and business expansion initiatives.
Effendy graduated in electronic engineering from University College London and later completed executive development programmes at Harvard Business School, INSEAD and Columbia Business School.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: [UPDATED] Victor Chin was among 10 detained in Op Viking money laundering sweep
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Businessman Victor Chin Boon Loong is among 10 people detained for alleged involvement in money laundering activities under Op Viking, which was carried out between Oct 15 last year and Jan 13.
Bukit Aman Anti-Money Laundering (Amla) investigation team chief Datuk Fazlisyam Abd Majid said Chin was arrested in December last year and later released on police bail.
"We do not rule out the possibility of recalling him to have his statement recorded. This will depend on any new evidence that requires further clarification. Any recall will be conducted in accordance with standard procedures," he said at a press conference today.
It was reported earlier this month that luxury watches, jewellery, cash — including foreign currencies — and two cars were seized during raids on several premises linked to Chin.
The raids form part of an ongoing money laundering investigation.
Police have also launched inquiries into allegations of a "corporate mafia" connected to Chin, with several premises already raided.
Inspector-General of Police Datuk Seri Mohd Khalid Ismail confirmed that authorities were taking the claims of a "corporate mafia" seriously and that enforcement action was already under way.
Police and Securities Commission Malaysia (SC) officials conducted raids at Chin's residence and other premises, seizing vehicles owned by his company.
Last week, Communications Minister Datuk Fahmi Fadzil said that the cabinet had directed police and other agencies, including the Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission, to investigate alleged "corporate manipulation".
The case is being investigated under Section 4(1) of the Anti-Money Laundering, Anti-Terrorism Financing and Proceeds of Unlawful Activities Act 2001.
Authorities have also advised the public to remain vigilant and to avoid suspicious financial activities as part of broader efforts to curb money laundering in the country.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: [UPDATED] RM470mil in assets seized in Op Viking money laundering probe
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Police have seized and frozen assets worth nearly half a billion ringgit in a major breakthrough in the ongoing investigation into a sophisticated money laundering syndicate under Op Viking.
The Bukit Aman Anti-Money Laundering (Amla) investigation team said the case, opened in October last year following intelligence gathered since 2018, involved a complex web of offences ranging from illegal moneylending and stock market manipulation to corporate disclosure violations.
Between Oct 15 and Jan 13, police arrested 10 people believed to be directly linked to the syndicate, comprising seven men and three women, including businessman Victor Chin.
Bukit Aman Amla chief Datuk Fazlisyam Abd Majid said investigations revealed the group was allegedly involved in offences under the Moneylenders Act 1951, Capital Markets and Services Act 2007, and Companies Act 2016.
This included issuing misleading loan agreements, threatening borrowers, and manipulating the stock market.
Authorities froze assets comprising eight properties, 32 vehicles, and shares owned by two companies.
"Police also seized 2.98 billion units of shares with a market value of RM323.6 million, along with trading accounts worth RM64.2 million and 404 bank accounts totalling RM85.6 million.
"We have also confiscated luxury items including cars, motorcycles, watches, jewellery, branded bags and cash in both local and foreign currencies," he told a press conference today.
A total of 41 people have had their statements recorded as investigations continue.
Fazlisyam said that police remained committed to pursuing those involved, directly or indirectly, in handling illicit funds.
"Investigations are being conducted based on facts and evidence obtained, with all actions taken in accordance with the law and established procedures," he said.
He also urged the public to come forward with information related to assets or proceeds linked to the case, highlighting that public cooperation was crucial in combating financial crimes.
The case is being investigated under Section 4(1) of the Anti-Money Laundering, Anti-Terrorism Financing and Proceeds of Unlawful Activities Act 2001.
Police also advised the public to remain vigilant and avoid suspicious financial activities as part of wider efforts to curb money laundering in the country.
It was
reported
earlier this month that luxury watches, jewellery, cash including foreign currencies, and two cars were seized during raids on premises linked to Chin.
The raids form part of a broader probe into a "corporate mafia" network allegedly associated with him.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Selangor exco wants maximum jail, caning for fatal drink-driving offences
Article:
SHAH ALAM: A Selangor state executive councillor has called for tougher penalties, including maximum jail terms and caning, for drink-driving offences following a fatal crash in Klang yesterday.
State Human Resources and Poverty Eradication Committee chairman V. Papparaidu said existing measures appeared insufficient as a deterrent, citing the rising number of drink-driving cases.
"I urge firm and commensurate legal action against the perpetrator. This is no longer an isolated case, as far too many such tragedies have occurred.
"It is time for a tougher approach through stricter enforcement and heavier punishment to serve as a lesson to the public. Appropriate assistance should also be extended promptly to the victim's family, especially when the deceased was the head of the household, in the interest of justice and preventing such tragedies from recurring.
"The lives of the public must not be put at risk due to the irresponsible actions of a few. Roads must be safe for all… enough is enough. Firm action must be prioritised for everyone's safety," he said in a Facebook post.
Yesterday, Klang Selatan district police chief Assistant Commissioner Lim Jit Huey said a motorcyclist was killed after being hit by a car driven by an individual under the
influence of alcohol and drugs
on Jalan Raya Barat.
He said the incident, which occurred about 11.40am, resulted in the victim, a man in his 30s, being flung onto the roof of another car. He was pronounced dead at the scene.
This morning, the Klang magistrate's court ordered the suspect, in his 20s, to be
remanded for four days
until Thursday to assist investigations under Section 44(1) of the Road Transport Act 1987 and Section 15(1)(a) of the Dangerous Drugs Act 1952.
Papparaidu also extended his condolences to the victim's family and prayed they would be given strength in facing the ordeal.
"I express my deepest sorrow over the tragic accident on Jalan Raya Barat, Klang, which claimed the life of an innocent motorcyclist, who was also a father of two young children.
"This incident once again highlights the dangers of driving under the influence of alcohol and drugs. It is even more heartbreaking when such negligence claims the life of an entirely innocent individual," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 'Bullets fell like rubber leaves': Ex-cop recalls deadly ambush
Article:
BACHOK: The memory of watching two comrades fall in a hail of bullets remains vivid for former Police Field Force personnel Hassan Jaafar, even after decades.
Hassan, 79, recalled the harrowing ambush in 1974 in Bentong, Pahang, when he was serving as a constable in a platoon deployed for anti-communist operations in a hilly, forested area.
"We were heading back to our tactical base in a Land Rover when, about 20 minutes later, we were ambushed.
"An inspector and a fellow constable were shot dead in front of me. Their bodies were riddled with bullets," he said when met at his home in Kampung Padang Sekolah here.
Hassan said the attack unfolded rapidly, with gunfire raining down relentlessly on the team.
"The bullets came non-stop, like rubber leaves falling. We took cover wherever we could and stayed on standby for about 15 minutes before responding," he said.
Despite the shock and fear, Hassan said the loss of his comrades only strengthened his resolve to fight on.
"Seeing them fall did not weaken my spirit. It made me more determined to survive and continue the fight.
"We eventually retreated towards the border area before seeking medical assistance. Several others were injured, some severely, including those who lost limbs," he said.
The fallen personnel were later brought back to the tactical base, while the injured were rushed to a nearby hospital for treatment.
Hassan served 12 years with the Police Field Force, including four years with the Eighth Battalion based in Simpang Renggam, Johor, before opting for early retirement in 1981.
Now a contractor, he said the incident continued to shape his life and outlook.
"Even after all these years, I remember every moment. It reminds me of the sacrifices made and the bond we shared as brothers in uniform," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: [UPDATED] Glove stocks stage surprise rally, Top Glove leads
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Shares of Bursa Malaysia's top four glove makers staged a surprise rally today, shrugging off a soft start at the opening bell.
The rally also lifted the Bursa Malaysia Healthcare Index to its highest level in over a month, rising 43.81 points or 2.92 per cent to 1,544.02, a level last seen on Feb 23.
Top Glove Corp Bhd, which ranked second on Bursa's most active list, rose 10.53 per cent or six sen to 63 sen, with 93.8 million shares traded, its busiest session in more than five months.
At 63 sen, the country's largest glovemaker was valued at RM5.18 billion.
Earlier, the stock opened 0.88 per cent or half a sen lower at 56.5 sen before climbing 13.27 per cent or 7.5 sen to an intraday high of 64 sen.
Hartalega Holdings Bhd followed closely, rising 13 per cent or 13 sen to RM1.13, with 51.93 million shares changing hands, its highest volume in more than a year.
The counter opened flat at RM1 before surging as much as 20 per cent or 20 sen to a high of RM1.20. It was the seventh most actively traded stock on Bursa Malaysia.
At RM1.13, the country's second-largest glovemaker had a market capitalisation of RM3.87 billion.
Kossan Rubber Industries Bhd also joined the rally, gaining 5.83 per cent or six sen to RM1.09, with 32.6 million shares traded, its busiest day since Feb 20, 2025.
The stock opened slightly lower at RM1.02 before climbing as much as 11.76 per cent or 12 sen to RM1.14, before paring some gains. Its market capitalisation stood at RM2.81 billion.
Meanwhile, Supermax Corp Bhd, the smallest among the "big four", rose 13.21 per cent or 3.5 sen to 30 sen, giving it a market capitalisation of RM961.28 million.
The stock hit an intraday high of 32.5 sen, up 22.64 per cent or six sen after opening flat at 26.5 sen.
It was the 18th most actively traded counter, with 32.67 million shares changing hands, its highest volume in about four months.
Trading volumes among Bursa's "big four" glove makers often mirror one another, but what is driving the renewed interest in the sector remains unclear.
Tradeview Capital fund manager Neoh Jia Man said the surge in glove counters appeared unusual.
He said the rally could be partly driven by customers rushing to build inventory amid concerns over further price increases, following shortages of key raw materials such as nitrile latex.
Against this backdrop, some glove manufacturers may also be taking the opportunity to raise prices beyond the increase in input costs, he added.
"While this could support margins in the near term, we believe such gains are unlikely to be sustainable, given the still-challenging competitive dynamics within the glove industry," Neoh said.
Last week, the Malaysian Rubber Glove Manufacturers Association called on the government to introduce temporary relief measures to address a severe shortage of nitrile butadiene rubber (NBR) latex.
As NBR is derived from petroleum and is a key raw material for nitrile gloves, the ongoing Middle East conflict has directly affected both its availability and cost.
CGS International Securities Malaysia Sdn Bhd analyst William Wo said Top Glove can offset raw material cost pressures through strategic price adjustments.
He said Top Glove's second-quarter core net profit of RM30.6 million fell short of expectations due to lower operating efficiency, though its net profit surged more than 100 per cent year-on-year to RM69.1 million for the first half of financial year 2026.
Input costs for nitrile and natural rubber spiked sharply in mid-March due to supply disruptions across South Korea, Thailand and China, with NBR inventory dropping to a record low of two weeks.
In light of this, Top Glove plans to raise average selling prices by US$7 to US$9 for nitrile gloves and US$2 to US$3 for natural rubber gloves to pass on higher costs to customers.
CGS International said this approach could protect margins while leveraging product substitution effects to maintain volume in a supply-constrained environment.
The firm maintained an "Add" rating on Top Glove with an unchanged target price of 67 sen, citing its lean cost structure, operational agility and versatile product portfolio.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Cambodia passes tough anti-scam law, life jail for kingpins
Article:
PHNOM PENH: The National Assembly of Cambodia today passed a draft law on combating online scams, which will deliver up to 30 years or life imprisonment to scam bosses, reported Xinhua.
A total of 112 lawmakers in attendance unanimously approved the draft Bill.
According to the Bill, online scam bosses will face between 15 and 30 years or life imprisonment if their operations lead to one or many deaths.
Ringleaders of online scam centres will face five to 10 years' prison and a fine of up to 1 billion riels (RM1 million). If their operations involve violence, torture, illegal confinement, human trafficking, or forced labour, they could face 10 to 20 years' jail and a fine of up to 2 billion riels.
Online scammers, meanwhile, will face two to five years' prison with a fine of up to 500 million riels.
Deputy Prime Minister and Justice Minister Koeut Rith said Cambodia was one of many countries in the region where criminals had operated online scams.
"This crime has not only seriously affected public security and order, but also badly damaged Cambodia's reputation and image on the international stage," he told the parliament.
Koeut Rith said the law would "enhance the effectiveness of the fight against online scams, aiming at safeguarding security and public order as well as enhancing the effectiveness of cooperation in combating online scams."
The draft Bill will need to be finally reviewed by the Senate before being submitted to King Norodom Sihamoni for promulgation.
The kingdom has launched an unprecedented nationwide crackdown on cyber scam networks to maintain social security, safety, and public order, and to restore the kingdom's image on the international stage.
The Southeast Asian country is committed to eradicating all online scam centres by April this year.
Deputy Prime Minister and Interior Minister Sar Sokha said in February that Cambodia had deported more than 30,000 suspected foreign scammers, as over 210,000 others had voluntarily left the kingdom after operations against online scams intensified since June 2025. – BERNAMA-XINHUA
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Deadly drink-driving: A look at recent cases
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Fatal accidents linked to drink-driving are under the spotlight once again following a
crash in Klang
yesterday, which claimed the life of a motorcyclist.
In 2020, the Road Transport Act was amended to introduce harsher penalties, including increased jail terms, fines and driving bans for such offences.
Notably, punishment under Section 44(1) for driving under the influence (DUI) of alcohol or drugs causing death or injury now sees first-time offenders facing up to 15 years in jail, a RM100,000 fine, and a 10-year driving ban.
The amendments also lowered legal alcohol limits to align with World Health Organization (WHO) standards. Despite these measures, fatal cases continue to be reported nationwide.
The New Straits Times looks back at recent drink-driving cases.
2026
In February, a married couple were burnt to death in Temerloh, Pahang, after their Proton Preve caught fire following a collision with a Porsche Cayenne driven by a man suspected of being intoxicated.
The accused, Shaun Lau Wee Hong, 28, pleaded not guilty. He was released on RM10,000 bail, with April 7 fixed for mention.
In January, a 67-year-old pedestrian was killed in Tanjung Tokong, Penang, after being struck by an SUV driven by a foreigner believed to have been drink-driving. The suspect has since been released on police bail while investigations continue.
2025
In August, a police constable died in Seremban after his motorcycle was hit by a driver suspected of being under the influence along Jalan Temiang. The accused, K. Kelvin, 27, pleaded not guilty and was released on RM5,000 bail.
Also in August, a 41-year-old jogger in Tawau died seven days after being hit by a suspected drunk driver. Initial tests indicated the driver of the four-wheel-drive vehicle was under the influence of alcohol.
In April, a food delivery rider was killed in Johor Baru after being struck by a BMW driven by a man believed to be intoxicated. The victim was hit while assisting another accident victim. A breathalyser test confirmed the driver was under the influence.
Updates on these cases have not been reported.
2024
In June, a factory operator in Johor Baru claimed trial at the magistrate's court to a charge of driving with excessive alcohol content, resulting in the death of a 17-year-old motorcyclist.
The accused, L. Satish Kumar, 25, was driving a Mitsubishi Xpander with an alcohol level of 173mg per 100ml of blood. The court allowed bail of RM7,000 and ordered him to report to the Kangkar Pulai police station once a month. His driving licence was suspended pending the disposal of the case.
2022
In December, a 20-year-old man died in Kuching after the car he was in crashed into a tree. Police found the driver to be under the influence of alcohol.
2020
In Sibu, a man drove against the flow of traffic while intoxicated, causing the death of a motorcyclist. He was later sentenced to eight years' imprisonment, fined RM20,000, and disqualified from holding a driving licence for 10 years.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: One man, his dog, and ChatGPT: Australia's AI vaccine saga
Article:
HONG KONG: Desperate to help his sick dog, one Australian man went down the ultimate ChatGPT research hole, using artificial intelligence to design a personalised experimental treatment and finding top scientists to administer it.
Paul Conyngham's months-long quest to fight his rescue mutt Rosie's cancer has grabbed the attention of OpenAI boss Sam Altman, who called it an "amazing story" in an X post on Friday.
Sydney-based AI consultant Conyngham told AFP that eight-year-old Rosie's mast cell cancer is now in partial remission and her biggest tumour has shrunk dramatically.
"She regained a lot of mobility and function" after receiving a custom mRNA vaccine along with powerful immunotherapy in December, he said.
Conyngham does not call his findings a cure – but experts unrelated to the dogged endeavour said they highlight AI's potential to accelerate medical research.
"I would have conversations and just keep them going non-stop" with ChatGPT, Gemini and Grok to study cancer therapies in-depth, Conyngham said.
Following the chatbots' advice, he paid $3,000 to have Rosie's genome sequenced, and used the same online tools to analyse her DNA data.
Next, he turned to AlphaFold, a scientific AI model that won 2024's chemistry Nobel, to better understand one of the mutated doggy genes.
Conyngham sought the help of a University of New South Wales (UNSW) team – also thanks to a ChatGPT recommendation – and other academics in Australia who made his research a reality.
'JUST A RASH'
Rosie's cancer was misdiagnosed for nearly a year, Conyngham said on the phone during one of the long daily walks the pair have resumed.
"I took her to the vet three times. And two times, the vet said, don't worry about it, it's just a rash," he said.
But Rosie got sicker, and a biopsy showed in 2024 that she did have terminal cancer.
Having tried chemotherapy, standard immunotherapy and surgery, costs were mounting, and Conyngham wanted more options.
So he used AI to delve deep into the world of emerging treatments including mRNA vaccines, which train the body's immune system and were widely used during the Covid pandemic.
"This was not a clinical trial by any means" and "it's not that AI cured cancer", said UNSW professor Martin Smith, who sequenced Rosie's genome for Paul.
"It was really driven by his determination to help his dog."
The combination of "three different disruptive technologies: genome sequencing, artificial intelligence, and RNA therapeutics… offers new possibilities and challenges", Smith said.
AI PROMISE
Chatbots also assisted Conyngham in navigating the reams of paperwork for ethical approval.
And through his new scientific network, he met a professor at the University of Queensland able to administer the fine-tuned treatment.
Not all the tumours responded as well as the largest one, however. Rosie has had to have another operation since, and it's unclear how long she has left to live.
"The short answer is we don't know for sure" what actually led to the reduction in size of Rosie's biggest tumour, said Pall Thordarson, director of UNSW's RNA institute which created the vaccine.
"He used the AI program… to design the actual mRNA sequence. And then he gave that information to us," Thordarson explained.
"AI holds lots of promise to improve and accelerate our research strategies," Nick Semenkovich at the Medical College of Wisconsin, unrelated to the Rosie saga, told AFP.
But UNSW and Conyngham "haven't published scientific details outside of their press release and interviews, so we don't know enough about the vaccine to understand how much AI helped in its development – or if the vaccine worked the way it was designed", Semenkovich said.
Patrick Tang Ming-kuen, a professor from The Chinese University of Hong Kong, said AI-powered research could help pets and humans survive, although the risk of errors is real.
"AI transforms a 'needle-in-a-haystack' search into a data-driven selection process, drastically shortening the timeframe between diagnosis and vaccine construction," he said.
Since Conyngham's story went global, Smith said his team have been fielding various new requests.
"You know: my cat's got a disease, my dog's got a disease, my aunt has got a disease."
But "it's hard for us to be able to help", he said. "There's a lot of things that have to align."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: MMEA rescues 13 tourists after boat stalls at sea
Article:
KUALA TERENGGANU: The Malaysian Maritime Enforcement Agency (MMEA) rescued 13 tourists after their boat stalled off Kuala Besut yesterday afternoon.
State MMEA director Captain Hamiludin Che Awang said the group, which included 12 foreigners, was rescued by an MMEA team on a routine patrol.
He said the patrol team spotted the boat, approximately 7.3 nautical miles from the Kuala Besut estuary, drifting, and passengers waving for help.
The boat had suffered engine damage.
Hamiludin said all 13 passengers - four from China, three from France, two each from the United Kingdom and Singapore, and one each from Bangladesh and Malaysia - were brought to the Besut MMEA jetty.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: KJ's suit against Jamal to be heard in Sessions Court
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The High Court here today ruled that former Health Minister Khairy Jamaluddin's application to file a lawsuit against Sungai Besar Umno division chief Jamal Yunos must be heard at the Sessions Court.
Judge Datuk Anand Ponnudurai made the decision on the grounds that claims filed in the High Court must involve amounts exceeding RM1 million.
Lawyer V. Mugunthan, representing Jamal as the defendant, said his client was prepared to issue an apology, although the plaintiff was also seeking damages.
"The judge ordered that the suit be transferred to the Sessions Court, as it is unlikely the plaintiff would be awarded damages exceeding RM1 million," he told reporters after the proceedings.
Earlier, lawyer Sheetal Kaur said the plaintiff was prepared to accept any amount proposed by the defendant, although no figure had been agreed upon.
"We are ready to accept whatever amount is offered, but at this stage, no sum has been determined. The defendant is only willing to apologise, whereas the case has drawn attention as the plaintiff is a former minister and politician," she said.
On Sept 19, 2023, Jamal filed an application to strike out the suit, citing, among other grounds, that the claim against him was unclear, embarrassing and an abuse of the court process.
Khairy, popularly known as KJ, filed the suit on May 24, 2023, saying that during the 2022 Umno General Assembly on Jan 14, 2023, Jamal held a press conference at the World Trade Centre Kuala Lumpur attended by media practitioners, Umno delegates, as well as party members and leaders.
The plaintiff alleged that on the same day, Jamal uploaded a video titled "I hope Khairy Jamaluddin will be subjected to action by the Umno Disciplinary Board after making reckless statements that embarrass Umno #UMNODuluKiniDanSelamanya" on Facebook under the account "DS Jamal Yunos (DS Jamal Yunos Official)".
The former Umno Youth chief said the defendant also uploaded the same recording on a TikTok account under the name "jamal_yunos".
Khairy further alleged that the defamatory statements implied, among other things, that he was a leader without integrity, unfit to lead, and disloyal to Umno and its leadership.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: World Bank Group's IFC, OCI TerraSus team up to advance Malaysia's semiconductor push
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The World Bank Group, via its private-sector arm the International Finance Corporation (IFC), has partnered with OCI TerraSus Sdn Bhd (OCI TRS), a unit of South Korea's OCI Holdings Company Ltd, to advance Malaysia's participation in the global semiconductor supply chain and create jobs in the country.
They said the partnership would also drive enhancements in IFC's environmental, social, and governance (ESG) standards and management systems.
"Central to this collaboration is the development of a clean energy-powered manufacturing facility for ultra-high purity semiconductor-grade polysilicon in Bintulu, Sarawak, through a joint venture with Japan's Tokuyama Corporation.
"Through this partnership with IFC and expansion in the global semiconductor supply chain, OCITRS targets active enhancements to its corporate governance and management systems, ESGcapabilities, technical advancements, competitiveness and long-term resilience," they said in a joint statement.
They said that under this collaboration, IFC will provide OCI TRS with a loan facility of up to US$125 million (US$1=RM4.01), which will primarily be used to construct the state-of-the-art manufacturing facility, the first of its kind in Southeast Asia.
OCI Holdings and OCI TerraSus chairman Lee Woo Hyun said the partnership is a key driver in strengthening management systems, ESG practices and long-term competitiveness as a global company.
"As demand grows for semiconductors and artificial intelligence, the importance of high-purity materials will continue to increase," he said.
World Bank Group country manager for Malaysia Judith Green said Malaysia has significant ambitions to move up the value chain in value-added manufacturing industries, which are major job creators, making them a key area of focus for the group.
"We are delighted to support the country towards fulfilling these ambitions. We are also encouraged by OCI TerraSus' commitment to strengthening its ESG standards, and look forward to supporting the company in achieving its commitments," she said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Philippine peso hits record low, Indonesian rupiah steadies after support steps
Article:
BENGALURU: The Philippine peso hit a record low on Monday and the Indonesian rupiah stopped short of touching a historic trough of 17,000 per dollar as the country's central bank introduced a new tool for banks to manage foreign-currency liquidity.
The Philippine peso hit a low of 60.776 per dollar, while stocks in Manila tumbled 2.5 per cent, as the net oil importergrapples with an "imminent danger of a critically low energy supply."
A number of oil-importing countries in the region have been hit hard by the US-Israeli war on Iran, which has effectively closed off the Strait of Hormuz, a key transit point for Middle Eastern oil.
Indonesia, another oil-importing country, continues to experience massive outflows due to concerns about the impact of elevated oil prices, compounding persistent concerns over fiscal and governance risks.
Jakarta stocks fell more than 2 per cent, extending monthly losses to around 14 per cent and putting them on course for their worst month since March 2020.
Foreign investors have pulled out 21.37 trillion rupiah (US$1.26 billion) from Indonesia's stock markets so far this month, the largest outflow in at least over a decade, according to LSEG data.
Its currency, the rupiah, wallowed at around 16,900 a dollar, a stone's throw from its record low of 17,000. Bank Indonesia on Monday implemented new foreign exchange repo transactions using its FX-denominated securities, providing banks with an alternative for liquidity management, particularly foreign exchange liquidity.
"The instruments function as high-quality, short-term collateral: Banks obtain the dollars they need, repay the loan when cash returns, while the collateral stays on the balance sheet," said Wei Li, head of multi-asset investments at BNP Paribas.
"The repo's market-neutral design limits immediate pressure on the IDR/USD rate, unlike a direct spot sale that would inject rupiah liquidity." Elsewhere in emerging markets, the Indian rupee rallied sharply to 93.85 per dollar after the central bank tightened limits on banks' foreign exchange positions.
Malaysia's benchmark stock index dipped 1.5 per cent, while stocks in South Korea and Taiwan fell as much as 5.3 per cent and 2.4 per cent. The conflict in the Middle East and its impact on global energy prices and supply continues to roil financial markets worldwide.
Oil-importing Asian emerging economies are particularly exposed, with higher prices already triggering capital outflows and pressuring currencies.
Foreign investors have pulled 34.71 billion baht (US$1.06 billion) out of Thai equities, while stock markets in South Korea and Taiwan have seen outflows worth US$19.71 billion and US$25.74 billion, respectively, exchange and LSEG data showed.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Caretaker jailed over neglect linked to Baby Syifaa's death
Article:
SEREMBAN: A caretaker was sentenced to one year's imprisonment by the Sessions Court here today for neglect that led to the death of 15-month-old Nour Rania Asyifaa Yuseri, known as Baby Syifaa, four years ago.
Judge N Kanageswari handed down the sentence to Khairunnisa Ahmad Damanhuri, 41, at the conclusion of proceedings involving the incident at a childcare centre in Bandar Sri Sendayan.
The court also imposed a RM15,000 fine, with an additional four months' imprisonment in default. Khairunnisa must complete 48 hours of community service within six months after serving her jail term, and is subject to a one-year good behaviour bond of RM5,000, to be secured by a family member.
Khairunnisa was represented by lawyer S Kavittha, while the prosecution was led by Negeri Sembilan State Public Prosecutor Ku Hayati Ku Haron. Baby Syifaa's parents, Anida Idrus, 44, and Yuseri Yusoff, 42, were present in court.
According to the charge, Khairunnisa, as the person entrusted with the child's care, had neglected her in a manner likely to cause physical injury. The offence was alleged to have occurred about 12.30pm on Feb 22, 2022.
She was charged under Section 31(1)(a) of the Child Act 2001, which provides for a maximum penalty of 20 years' imprisonment, a fine of up to RM50,000, or both.
On July 31, 2024, the Coroner's Court ruled that the child's death was caused by neck compression after she was found unconscious in her cot at the centre.
A total of 28 witnesses, including the child's parents, testified during the inquest, which began on Sept 22, 2022.
Yuseri had told the court that his daughter's death was not caused by her head being caught in the cot's fabric, but by strangulation.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Muar student awaiting SPM results dies in crash with bus at Parit Jawa traffic light junction
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, March 30 — An 18-year-old student awaiting his Sijil Pelajaran Malaysia (SPM) results died after colliding with a bus at the Parit Jawa traffic light junction in Muar, Johor, yesterday.
The sole child of his family sustained severe injuries and was pronounced dead at the scene, according to a report published by
Berita Harian
.
A video of the crash circulating on social media showed the student’s motorcycle hitting the side of a turning bus before overturning.
The victim, a former student of Sekolah Menengah Agama (SMA) Ad-Diniah Parit Subari, Jalan Parit Yusuf, was reportedly on his way to work, not far from the location of the accident.
Muar district police chief, Assistant Commissioner Raiz Mukhliz Azman Aziz, said preliminary investigations indicated the bus, coming from Batu Pahat towards Muar town, had stopped at the traffic lights.
“As the lights turned green, the bus driver signalled a left turn when the student’s motorcycle collided with the left side of the bus. The victim fell and sustained serious head and body injuries before paramedics confirmed his death,” he said in a statement.
The case is being investigated under Section 41(1) of the Road Transport Act 1987.

Title: Lorry driver in Batu Pahat pleads not guilty to slapping, punching and kicking wife for leaving home without permission
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, March 30 — A 39-year-old lorry driver pleaded not guilty at the Magistrates’ Court in Batu Pahat, Johor, today to charges of assaulting his 40-year-old wife after she left their home without his knowledge.
Mohamed Hamka Salleh, father of six, entered the plea before Magistrate Arun Noval Dass, according to
Harian Metro
.
He is accused of deliberately causing injury by slapping his wife’s face, punching her chest, and kicking her legs at their home in Kampung Linau Besar, Bukit Pasir, at 6.30pm on March 25.
The charges were framed under Section 323 of the Penal Code read together with Section 326A, which carry a maximum penalty of one year’s imprisonment or a fine of up to RM2,000, or both, while Section 326A doubles the maximum jail term for Section 323 offences.
According to reports, the argument escalated as the accused repeatedly slapped, punched, and kicked his wife.
Fearful of further harm, the victim lodged a police report at the Batu Pahat District Police Headquarters.
Mohamed Hamka was arrested the following day at 1.59pm to assist with the investigation.
The prosecution was handled by Deputy Public Prosecutor Firdaus Ruslan, while the accused was unrepresented.
The court allowed bail of RM2,000 with one surety, ordered him to report to the nearest police station every three weeks, and prohibited him from contacting the victim or witnesses until the case concludes.
The case is set to be mentioned again on May 14 for document submission and appointment of the accused’s lawyer.

Title: Government ready to roll out cloud seeding as prolonged heat grips several states nationwide
Article:
SHAH ALAM, March 30 — The Ministry of Natural Resources and Environmental Sustainability (NRES) is prepared to implement intervention measures, including cloud seeding, to mitigate the impact of the prolonged hot weather currently affecting several states nationwide.
Minister Datuk Seri Arthur Joseph Kurup said the measures would be carried out based on prevailing needs, guided by data and technical reports from relevant agencies to ensure the impact of the weather phenomenon is effectively managed, particularly in sectors such as agriculture.
“If necessary, we will carry out interventions such as cloud seeding based on available data to ensure that the measures taken are timely and effective,” he told reporters after attending the Forest Conservation Certificate (FCC) as Malaysia’s Trusted Platform for Conservation Finance programme here today.
Arthur said the Malaysian Meteorological Department (MetMalaysia) would continue to play a key role in providing accurate weather information to support follow-up actions, while helping relevant agencies and the public make the necessary preparations.
“Given the increasingly unpredictable climate conditions, we have an important role in providing early warnings, particularly to agencies such as the National Disaster Management Agency (NADMA) and the Department of Agriculture, so that they can make the necessary preparations,” he said.
Previously, MetMalaysia said that the hot weather phenomenon currently affecting the country is expected to persist until the onset of the Southwest Monsoon in June, due to drier atmospheric conditions and lower rainfall. — Bernama

Title: Give Malaysia’s tour buses 3,000 litres diesel subsidy, travel industry urges govt amid soaring prices
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, March 30 — The Malaysian government should offer diesel subsidies to tour buses and tour vans amid an 82 per cent jump in local retail diesel prices due to the Iran war, Malaysian Inbound Tourism Association (MITA) urged today.
MITA president Mint Leong said local tourism transport operators are struggling to cope with and absorb the increased costs from the diesel price hike, as most of their vehicles such as tour buses and tour vans use diesel.
“MITA wishes to request to the government, if subsidies are given to the tourism industry, 3,000 litre per month for tour buses and 2,500 litre per month for tour vans,” she said in a press conference here, adding that this was a reasonable amount based on calculations from the local travel industry.
With the diesel retail price jumping from RM3.02 per litre from before the war to RM5.52 litre now, Leong said the local tourism industry has heard feedback of those tour bus and tour van operators who want to give up and shut down their businesses, as well as ferry operators who have already reduced trips to cope with increased costs.
“I want to call all industry players, don’t give up, don’t stop, however much we can “tahan” (stand it), we stand, but we still hope for the government to give the subsidy we request,” she said.
MITA vice-president (transport) Logeswaran K. Sukumar urged the government to not abandon the local tourism players whom he said are the ‘backbone’ of tourism in Malaysia. — Picture by Raymond Manuel
In the same press conference, MITA vice-president (transport) Logeswaran K. Sukumar said the diesel subsidies that the travel industry is seeking from the government would not even cover the full costs from the rising diesel prices, but would at least cover part of their fuel costs.
“We don’t ask for much from the government. But what can we do? We are the second biggest contributor of revenue to the country, it can’t be can’t even give us a little. At least give us 3,000 litres,” he said.
While saying that the rising fuel prices is part of a global crisis, he urged the government to not abandon the local tourism players whom he said are the “backbone” of tourism in Malaysia.
MORE TO COME

Title: Police confirm Victor Chin detained in Ops Viking, RM473m assets seized in money‑laundering crackdown
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, March 30 — Police have confirmed that businessman Victor Chin Boon Loong was among 10 individuals detained on suspicion of involvement in money laundering activities under Ops Viking, which was conducted between October 15 last year and January 13 this year.
Bukit Aman AMLA (Anti-Money Laundering and Anti-Terrorism Financing) Investigation Unit chief Datuk Fazlisyam Abd Majid said Victor Chin was detained in December last year and has since been released on police bail, although he may be recalled if necessary.
Fazlisyam added that the remaining nine detainees, comprising six men and three women, believed to be directly linked to the investigation, have also been released on bail, while 40 others have been called in to provide statements.
He said the operation was launched on October 14 last year based on intelligence on money laundering activities dating back to 2018, with a formal investigation opened on Oct 25 under Section 4(1) of the Anti-Money Laundering, Anti-Terrorism Financing and Proceeds of Unlawful Activities Act 2001 (Act 613).
“This case involves several individuals suspected of committing money laundering offences through various methods, including the acquisition or ownership of publicly listed companies on Bursa Malaysia, as well as private companies,” he said at a press conference here today.
Fazlisyam highlighted that the operation resulted in the freezing and seizure of various assets, including eight properties, 32 vehicles, 1,100 shares in two private limited companies, Central Depository System (CDS) accounts totaling RM323,588,293, trading accounts amounting to RM64,246,417, and 404 bank accounts holding RM85,624,528.
He added that the investigation is being conducted thoroughly on all parties suspected of being directly or indirectly involved in managing funds derived from unlawful activities.
The probe is being carried out under Section 27B and Section 29B of the Moneylenders Act 1951, Section 176 of the Capital Markets and Services Act 2007 and Section 221 of the Companies Act 2016.
On March 16, the police confirmed that they had raided several premises belonging to Victor Chin, who is alleged to be involved in corporate manipulation linked to the Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission (MACC).
Separately, Fazlisyam noted that authorities have not received any report regarding Victor Chin’s claim that a member of Parliament demanded RM10 million to assist in resolving the corporate manipulation issue. — Bernama

Title: Malaysia’s tourism industry announces 70-80pc maximum price hike for tour buses, vans due to diesel pinch
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, March 30 — Tour bus and tour van operators will be able to increase their prices by up to 80 per cent, the Malaysian Inbound Tourism Association (MITA) announced today in a new price guideline effective from today.
MITA president Mint Leong announced the new price guide, which the local tourism industry has stressed today is crucial for its survival as diesel prices continue to climb up in Malaysia.
